coredata.dc:title: Dual-emission of Eu based metal-organic frameworks hybrids with carbon dots for ratiometric fluorescent detection of Cr(VI)
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yingying
He, Jiang
Zheng, Mingda
Qin, Mingda
Wei, Wei
coredata.dc:description: A novel blue fluorescence nitrogen and cobalt(II) co-doped carbon dots (CDs) encapsulated in europium metal-organic frameworks (CDs@Eu-MOFs) were synthesized to form a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for the detection of Cr(VI). The CDs@Eu-MOFs were characterized by transmission electron microscopic (TEM), scanning electron microscopic (SEM), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) techniques. The as-prepared CDs@Eu-MOFs not only kept the fascinating optical properties of CDs and Eu3+ to give dual-emission but also had good stability in an aqueous solution. In the presence of Cr(VI), the fluorescence intensity of the CDs decreased and the intensity of the Eu-MOFs remained. Under optimized conditions, the synthesized CDs@Eu-MOFs showed high sensitivity and selectivity with a linear range from 2 to 100 μM and a detection limit of 0.21 μM for the detection of Cr(VI). The possible mechanism is discussed. Meanwhile, the fluorescence sensor was successfully utilized to detection of Cr(VI) in natural samples with good recoveries ranges.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon Dots Eu-MOFs Cr(VI) Fluorescence sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of bare Eu-MOFs and CDs@Eu-MOFs
Fluorescence detection of Cr(VI) based on CDs@Eu-MOFs
Sensing mechanism of CDs@Eu-MOFs fluorescence sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CDs; (B) TEM and (C) SEM images of the CDs@Eu-MOFs; (D) FT-IR spectra of the Eu-MOFs (a), CDs (b), and CDs@Eu-MOFs (c); (E) XRD pattern of the Eu-MOFs (a) and CDs@Eu-MOFs (b); (F) UV–vis absorption spectra (blue line), excitation (black line) and emission spectra (red line) of CDs@Eu-MOFs.
The influences of buffer solution (A, B) pH and (C, D) temperature.

(A) The fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs under multifarious excitation wavelengths from 330 nm to 420 nm. (B) The fluorescence emission spectra of the 0.02 g CDs@Eu-MOFs before (black line) and after addition of 500 μM Cr(VI) (red line).
(A) The fluorescence spectra of the CDs@Eu-MOFs in the presence of different concentrations of Cr(VI). (B) Plot of the F467/F616 intensity against the different concentrations of Cr(VI).
The fluorescence selectivity spectra of the CDs@Eu-MOFs in the absence and presence of 100 μM Cr(VI) and various metal ions.
Stern-Volmer plot for the fluorescence of CDs quenched by different concentrations of Cr(VI).
The fluorescence decay curve of CDs (black line) and CDs combined with the Cr(VI) (red line) in aqueous solution at room temperature.
Schematic illustration of the synthetic route of the CDs, CDs@Eu-MOFs and mechanism for Cr(VI) detection.
Comparison of sensing performance of different fluorescent sensors for Cr(VI) detection.
Determination of Cr(VI) in actual samples using CDs@Eu-MOFs as a fluorescent sensor(n = 3).

head.ce:title: Dual-emission of Eu based metal-organic frameworks hybrids with carbon dots for ratiometric fluorescent detection of Cr(VI)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel blue fluorescence nitrogen and cobalt(II) co-doped carbon dots (CDs) encapsulated in europium metal-organic frameworks (CDs@Eu-MOFs) were synthesized to form a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for the detection of Cr(VI). The CDs@Eu-MOFs were characterized by transmission electron microscopic (TEM), scanning electron microscopic (SEM), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) techniques. The as-prepared CDs@Eu-MOFs not only kept the fascinating optical properties of CDs and Eu3+ to give dual-emission but also had good stability in an aqueous solution. In the presence of Cr(VI), the fluorescence intensity of the CDs decreased and the intensity of the Eu-MOFs remained. Under optimized conditions, the synthesized CDs@Eu-MOFs showed high sensitivity and selectivity with a linear range from 2 to 100 μM and a detection limit of 0.21 μM for the detection of Cr(VI). The possible mechanism is discussed. Meanwhile, the fluorescence sensor was successfully utilized to detection of Cr(VI) in natural samples with good recoveries ranges.

The ratiometric fluorescent detection of Cr(VI) has been designed based on Eu3+ functionalized metal-organic frameworks hybrids with carbon dots, the sensing mechanism of CDs@Eu-MOFs fluorescence sensor on the basis of the inner filter effect (IFE) could occur.
fx1

ce:keyword.ce:text: Carbon Dots Eu-MOFs Cr(VI) Fluorescence sensor

ce:section.ce:para: As a fatal toxicant, chromium is widely applied in automobile parts, leather ware, pigments, stainless steel, and other industrial products [1,2]. Cr(VI) species among the common heavy metal cation pollutants have attracted wide attention in recent years [3,4]. Because it belongs to carcinogenic and mutagenic ions that can cause hereditary genetic defects, allergic reactions, and kinds of cancers [5]. Therefore, they are classified as heavy contaminants by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency [6]. In the year of 2011, a severe empoisoned accident of chromium slag had taken place in Yunnan province of China, the concentration of Cr(VI) in the environment is far above the Chinese Integrated wastewater discharge standard [7,8]. Meanwhile, South Africa, which is the main resource of chromite, had reported that Cr(VI) has caused severe water pollution [9]. Moreover, according to Chinese National Standard of Food Safety, (the permitted maximum concentration of chromium in aquatic animals is 2 mg/kg) [10], however, the chromium concentration of wild marine fishes can be as high as 4.36 mg/kg (depending on species) in Hainan coastal areas (South China Sea) [11]. Consequently, detection of Cr(VI) is our top priority mission. The techniques such as ion chromatography (IC), atomic absorption spectrum (AAS), inductively coupled plasma mass spectrum (ICP-MS), spectrophotometry [12,13] have been applied to Cr(VI) detection. However, these technologies exist some drawbacks, such as high cost and complicated sample pretreatments. Thus, it is imperative to develop a simple and inexpensive method for detection of Cr(VI).

Fluorescence sensors have attracted great interests due to their high sensitivity and selectivity, low cost and technical simplicity, however, single-wavelength-based fluorescent sensors can be affected by concentration, environment and excitation intensity, which limit their applications [14–16]. Ratiometric fluorescence sensors can relieve these problems by measuring the ratio of two or more emission bands at different wavelengths [17–20], increase sensitivity and selectivity by comparison [21,22]. Nanomaterial fluorescent sensors, especially the luminescent metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) has been demonstrated as a new platform for chemical sensing because of their large specific surface area, strong encapsulation ability, controllable pore sizes, predictable nanostructures [23–25], multicolor and tunable emission properties [26]. Classically, two main tactics have been adopted in order to synthetize luminescent MOFs. One of the tactics is synthesis of MOFs using the lanthanide ions as the metal center which are able to emit strong photoluminescence (PL) emission, for the appropriate adjacent chromophores can form f-f or f-d energy transfer system with lanthanide ions by using UV excitation [27,28]. Among the all sorts of lanthanide ions, europium ion and terbium ion are the widest application because of the large Stokes shift and relative long lifetimes [29–31] and their complexes have advantages of fixed emission peaks and strong fluorescence intensity. In addition, the coordination number of lanthanide ions is also very high and variable, there are unusual coordination modes when combined with other elements. Carbon dots (CDs) as emerging luminescent materials have been regarded as a new star of luminescent materials due to their fascinating optical properties, easy preparation, low toxicity, chemical stability and biocompatibility [32–34]. Recently, an encapsulation tactic combines the advantages of MOFs and nanoparticles. Therefore, strong fluorescence CDs@MOFs is constructed for enhancing sensing for metal ions [35].

In this work, a novel blue light-emitting nitrogen and cobalt(II) co-doped CDs have been encapsulated in the Eu-MOFs in order to detect Cr(VI) [36]. The synthetic CDs@Eu-MOFs exhibited the strip nanostructures and very stable in aqueous solution. Besides, the porous nanostructures can improve the sensitivity of the sensor because the high specific surface can increase the contact area between the sensor and Cr(VI). The CDs@Eu-MOFs showed two emission peaks at 467 nm and 616 nm by excitation at 390 nm. The fluorescence of CDs would be quenched by Cr(VI), and the characteristic emission of Eu-MOFs did not change after adding Cr(VI), hence a ratiometric fluorescence sensor can be constructed. The ratiometric fluorescent sensor for Cr(VI) detection showed low detection limit, high sensitivity and good selectivity which was established and could be applied in natural samples.

All reagents were used analytical grade without further purification. Eu(NO3)3·6H2O was obtained from Chengdu Kelon Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China), 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylic acid (H3BTC), tartaric acid was purchased from Xi’an Chemistry Co. Led.(Xi’an, China). Potassium dichromate, sodium chloride and other reagents were obtained from Tianjin Guangfu Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water purified by a Millipore-Q system (18.2 MΩ cm−1).

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained by RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Ultraviolet visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were analyzed by TU-1901 UV–visible spectrophotometer (Purkinje, China). X-Ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were recorded using X′ Pert PRO diffractometer (Panalytical, Netherlands). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was observed using NEXUS 670 (Nicolet, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained by FEI Tecnai G2F30 (FEI Company, USA). The scanning electron microscopic (SEM) images were taken by an S-94 4800 (Hitachi, Japan). Time-resolved fluorescence spectra were carried out in a time-correlated single-photon-counting (TCSPC) system on a FL920P spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd, U.K.) with the excitation at 370 nm.

The CDs were synthesized according to the microwave method described in a previous report [37]. Typically, 0.5 g tartaric acid, 550 μL triethylenetetramine and Co(NO3)2·6H2O were dissolved in 5 mL H2O by stirring for 30 min. Then, the reaction precursors were heated for 4 min in a household microwave oven operating at a power of 700 W. After the reaction, the product was cooled down to room temperature naturally and then added 10 mL H2O to dissolve the product. Then the obtained solution was purified by dialyzing against pure water using a membrane (MW = 500 Da) for 12 h and then stored at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of bare Eu-MOFs and CDs@Eu-MOFs
ce:section.ce:para: The bare Eu-MOFs were synthesized using the same method except the CDs in the precursors. Briefly, 0.1 mM Eu(NO3)3·6H2O and 0.1 mM H3BTC were dissolved in 25 mL DMF, then 2 mL H2O and 100 μL CDs were added into the solution. The mixture was stirred for 30 min at room temperature and heated at 100 °C for 24 h to produce crystals. The products were collected, and washing with ethanol. Finally, the CDs@Eu-MOFs dried by a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 3 h.

In a typically assay, the 20 mg CDs@Eu-MOFs were added into 3 mL BR buffer (pH=7), oscillated for 5 min in order to obtain CDs@MOFs suspension, then the suspension was added into a quartz cuvette with different concentrations of Cr(VI). After 3 min, fluorescence emission spectra were recorded by exciting at 390 nm at room temperature. The selectivity experiment for Cr(VI) was confirmed by adding different ions instead of Cr(VI).

Water samples were gathered by the tap water, the Yellow River (Lanzhou, China) and the stream (Urumqi, China). Water samples were filtrated by the syringe filter (0.45 µm) and then spiked with various concentrations of Cr(VI). The fluorescence spectra were recorded when the 0.02 g CDs@Eu-MOFs were dispersed in the solution for a while.

As shown in Fig. 1A, the TEM images show the amorphous nature of CDs, and that particle size of the used CDs range from 4 to 12 nm. The SEM and TEM images reveal that the CDs@Eu-MOFs are exist as micron tetrahedron (Fig. 1B, C). The FTIR spectra (Fig. .1D) demonstrates that the peak at 3430 cm−1 belongs to stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H, the peak at 2930 cm−1 is in accordance with the stretching vibrations of C-H bond, the peaks at 1656 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of C-O and C-C bonds. The peak at 1384 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of C-N. Therefore, the prepared CDs@Eu-MOFs have characteristic absorption bands of both CDs and Eu-MOFs, the CDs have been capsulated into the Eu-MOFs. The XRD pattern of the CDs@Eu-MOFs is consistent well with that of the bare Eu-MOFs (Fig. 1E). As revealed in Fig. 1F, the UV–vis absorption spectra exhibits a strong absorption peak at 210 nm accompanied by a shoulder around 300 nm of CDs. The absorption peak at 210 nm is seen as the π-π* transition of C˭C bonds, the absorption peak at 300 nm possibly belongs to the n-π * transition of the amine groups on the surface of the CDs simultaneously [38,39]. The dilute CDs solution exhibits a strong emission peak at 467 nm with excitation of 390 nm, thus there is an intense blue fluorescence under UV excitation (λ = 365 nm). The prepared CDs@Eu-MOFs solution also has a strong blue fluorescence (467 nm) when the excited at 390 nm.

To assess the stability of CDs@Eu-MOFs and acquire high sensitivity and selectivity, various conditions of Cr(VI) detection were investigated. As we know, the CDs@Eu-MOFs materials are mainly affected by pH and temperature in practical application. The ratiometric fluorescence intensities (F467/F616) of CDs@Eu-MOFs in different pH were evaluated ( Fig. 2A). It is apparent that the F467/F616 is stable in the wide pH range of 2–10, indicating the CDs@Eu-MOFs can be used in acidic and alkaline conditions. Then the influence of temperature was also considered (Fig. 2B). The F467/F616 remains basically unchanged in the temperature range of 10–40°C. The good interference rejection capacity of CDs@Eu-MOFs to environmental is because the reference signal of materials will correct the above factors [40].

As shown in Fig. 3A, the fluorescence intensity of CDs gradually increased and weakened slowly when the excitation wavelengths increased from 330 nm to 420 nm. As shown in Fig. 3B, the CDs@Eu-MOFs suspension fluorescence sensor exhibits three emission peaks at 467 nm, 590 nm and 616 nm, respectively. The peak of 467 nm should belong to the encapsulated CDs, and the peaks of 590 nm and 616 nm should be classified with the characteristic excitation peak of Eu3+. Obviously, the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots is much stronger than that of the Eu3+. The fluorescence of CDs (467 nm) can be quenched by adding Cr(VI), whereas the fluorescence of Eu3+ has hardly changed. According to above analysis, in order to detect Cr(VI), we could construct a ratiometric fluorescence sensor utilizing the ratiometric fluorescence intensity of CDs@Eu-MOFs at 467 nm and 616 nm (F467/F616).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Cr(VI) based on CDs@Eu-MOFs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 4A, the fluorescence intensities of CDs@Eu-MOFs at 467 nm were weakened gradually with different concentrations of Cr(VI) from 0 μM to 500 μM, meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity at 616 nm kept mostly unchanged. According to previous reports, the fluorescent quenching at 467 nm may result from the inner filter effect of Cr(VI) [41]. This means the absorption of Cr(VI) shields the excitation or emission wavelengths of CDs@Eu-MOFs, leading to the fluorescence quenching at 467 nm [42]. The F467/F616 had a wide linear correlation with Cr(VI) concentration with range from 2 μM to 100 μM. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.21 μM (S/N = 3) (Fig. 4B), which was lower than the maximum allowable concentration (0.96 μM) of Cr(VI) in drinking water permitted by the WHO [43]. In contrast with the previously reported fluorescence sensors for detecting Cr(VI), CDs@Eu-MOFs were superior to the other Cr(VI) sensors ( Table 1). The good performance may be due to the uniform columnar structures of CDs@Eu-MOFs, which lead to a large specific surface area to react with Cr(VI) and can enrich the Cr(VI) for increasing sensitivity.

In general, the CDs@Eu-MOFs conforms to the requirement of fluorescent sensors. Furthermore, A variety of 100 μM metal ions were added into the CDs@Eu-MOFs aqueous solution in sequence, including Na+, K+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, NH4 +, Mn2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Al3+ and Cr2O7 2-, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5, there was no significant change in F467/F616 when the interfering metal ions were added, only a small descent of ratio in the presence of Cu2+, Fe3+and Hg2+was observed. The results evidently revealed the CDs@Eu-MOFs have excellent selectivity for the detection of Cr(VI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism of CDs@Eu-MOFs fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing mechanism of Cr(VI) by the CDs@Eu-MOFs fluorescence sensor is revealed in Scheme 1. The fluorescence of CDs (467 nm) can be quenched by adding Cr(VI), while the fluorescence of Eu3+ remains unchanged. Meanwhile, the relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the quencher concentration can be shown by the Stern–Volmer equation: F0/F = 1 + Ksv[Q], where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence of and presence of quencher, Ksv is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant and [Q] is the concentration of Cr(VI). As shown in Fig. 6, the Ksv is equal to 0.00556 μM−1. Hence, fluorescence lifetimes were measured in order to distinguish dynamic and static quenching. As revealed in Fig. 7, the lifetime of CDs and CDs combined with the Cr(VI) are basically consistent and the average lifetime τavg is 8.1 ns. Thus, it can be proved a static quenching. Due to the resonance energy transfer (RET) mechanism shows declined significantly of the donor fluorescence lifetime in the quenching process, it is likely that RET may not play an important role in our system [44]. According to previous reports, Cr(VI) exhibits broad absorption at 260 nm and 360 nm, the Cr(VI) can not merely efficiently shield the excitation light of CDs, but also absorb the emission light of CDs [37]. As a consequence, fluorescence quenching on the basis of the inner filter effect (IFE) could occur in a highly efficient way [41]. Compared with the RET mechanism, the sensing approach based on the IFE exhibit high sensitivity and fast response [44]. Eu-MOFs as a host for CDs can strongly adsorb and accumulate Cr(VI) ions, the synergetic effect of accumulation and quenching may effectively increase the sensitivity of the probe [35], and Meanwhile, the MOFs can also stabilize and disperse the CDs [45].

The CDs@Eu-MOFs for the determination of Cr(VI) in real samples from the tap river, the Yellow River and the steam were further explored. As listed in Table 2, the recoveries gathered by the tap water samples, the Yellow River water samples and the steam samples ranged from 91.90% to 110.62%. Besides, the relative standard deviation (RSD) of Cr(VI) was in the range of 0.69–1.57%. The results exhibited that the CDs@Eu-MOFs was credible and accurate. Consequently, the CDs@Eu-MOFs as a ratiometric fluorescence sensor can be successfully applied to the determination of Cr(VI) in practical samples.

In general, the porous CDs@Eu-MOFs have been successfully synthesized for detection of Cr(VI) by ratiometric fluorescence. The CDs@Eu-MOFs, as a ratiometric fluorescence sensor, exhibited good selectivity, high sensitivity, wide linear range and low detection limit, moreover, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor can effectively surmount the interference from environment, such as temperature, pH and other factors. Additionally, the special structure increased the stability and accuracy of the CDs@Eu-MOFs. The sensor can be applied to the detection of Cr(VI) in environmental samples. Therefore, the prepared CDs@Eu-MOFs have potential to detect Cr(VI) in drinking water.
